actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Each rank of Super-Speed gives you the effects of Improved Initiative as an Enhanced Trait, Quickness, and Speed, with a ground speed rank equal to your power rank.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: So with Super-Speed 10, for example, you have +40 to initiative checks, can perform routine actions normally requiring two hours in just 6 seconds, and have a ground speed of 2,000 miles per hour!

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Heroes with Super-Speed often have additional powers based on their speed, particularly things like Air Control (whipping up powerful winds, see Element Control, previously) or modifiers to their Strength Damage like Area or Multiattack to represent the ability to make a rapid series of attacks in a single turn.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: POWER STUNT Temporarily gain and use an Alternate Effect (see Alter- nate Effect in the Powers chapter).

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: RESISTANCE Gain an immediate additional resistance check against an ongoing effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: RETRY Certain effects (see the Powers chapter) require extra ef- fort to retry after a certain degree of failure.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The extra ef- fort merely permits another attempt to use the effect;

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Players can use various tokens (poker chips, glass beads, etc.) to keep track of their hero points, handing them over to the Gamemaster when they spend them.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: COST OF EXTRA EFFORT At the start of the turn immediately after using extra ef- fort, the hero becomes fatigued.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: A fatigued hero who uses extra effort becomes exhausted and an exhausted hero who uses extra effort is incapacitated.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: In essence, spending a hero point lets you use extra effort without suffering fatigue.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: HERO POINTS Whether it’s luck, talent, or sheer determination, heroes have something setting them apart from everyone else, allowing them to perform amazing feats under the most difficult circumstances.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The Gamemaster requires a hero point to add that detail and says the right chemicals are close at hand.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: How much players are allowed to “edit” circumstances is up to the individual Gamemaster, but generally hero points should not be allowed to change any event that has al- ready occurred or any detail already explained in-game.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: HEROIC FEAT You can spend a hero point to gain the benefits of one rank of a advantage you don’t already have until the end of your next turn (see the Advantages chapter).

actions.subject: DifficultyClass
actions.raw: use the rules for other effects as guidelines on how to handle these situations, using Transform rank to determine resistance DCs.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Keep in mind, however, that Transform has a sustained duration, which may affect how such “tricks” work (e.g., the trap disappears if the character is stunned, the suffo- cating gas dissipates unless the character concentrates each round to continue transforming it, etc.).

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: For an effect capable of wearing- down and eventually destroying objects, use Weaken Toughness instead.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: TRANSFORMING BEINGS Transforming living or otherwise animate beings as op- posed to inanimate matter requires an effect other than Transform.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: When dealing with non-living creatures (those ab- sent Stamina) capable of resistance checks (possessing Will) the GM may permit Transform to function like an Affliction against them.

actions.subject: Device
actions.raw: Transforming someone’s devices or equipment requires targeting them first:

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: So transforming a hand-held weapon like a gun requires an attack check and permits the wielder a Dodge re- sistance check with a +5 bonus (for a hand-held item).

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Targeting a worn suit of armor requires an attack check and allows the wearer a Dodge resistance check (with no modifier for a large item).

actions.subject: Device
actions.raw: Transform is generally just another way of “removing” a device or equipment, considered a part of their discount in cost, although transformed items should eventually be restored or replaced.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: 7 points per rank You can gain or use potentially any effect of the appropri- ate type and descriptor!

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: So you cannot, for example, acquire Enhanced Trait as a Variable effect to improve a trait beyond its power level limit, or acquire effects or descriptors the Gamemaster has specifically banned from the series.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The GM has final say as to whether or not a particular use of a Variable effect is appropriate and may veto your allocations, if necessary.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: The subject granted the use of the effect con- trols its configuration, if appropriate for its descrip- tors (although you retain the ability to withdraw use of the effect altogether whenever you wish).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: A Variable effect with Affects Others may have the Ranged extra to improve the range at which you can grant the effect to another.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.object: it from doing what it is supposed to do
actions.raw: To do otherwise would require weighing the effect down with numerous game-system limitations that would keep it from doing what it is supposed to do:

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The limits of power flexibility in MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS are deliberately set by Variable effects, the use of extra effort, and hero points.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Many comic book heroes who appear to have the power to “do anything” are actually using one of these options in MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS terms.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: However, generally speaking, these characters have certain abilities they use all the time (powers they have acquired with power points) and “stunts” they only do from time to time, essentially power stunts performed with extra effort (and possibly hero points).

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: most powers in the game have the potential to do “stunts” via extra effort, so the “variability” of Magic seen in the comics is already built-in to the system, with some costs to control it, without having to give players carte blanche to duplicate any effect in the game at will (which is just likely to slow things down and cause game balance issues).

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: The player can pre-build certain commonly used animal forms for use during play, but also has the option to new configurations that fit into the power’s descriptors.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: WEAKEN RESISTANCE CHECK Fortitude or Will vs.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The target loses power points from the affected trait equal to the difference between the check result and the DC, up to a maximum of the Weaken rank.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Multiple failed resistance checks against a Weaken effect are cumulative, up to a maximum of the Weaken rank, at which point the effect cannot weaken the trait further.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: WEAKENING ABILITIES Abilities weakened below a rank of –5 become debilitated.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Any further uses of Weaken on the subject have no effect until the ability recovers to a rank of at least –5.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: This also applies to equipment, although it tends to have fewer traits to weaken, and the GM should feel free to disallow any Weaken effects that don’t make reasonable sense.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: For example, just because a Weaken Damage effect is possible doesn’t mean a character should be able to cause guns to do less damage;

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Once you have hit with a Concentration Weaken, so long as you continue to take a standard action each turn to maintain the effect, the target must make a new resistance check against it, with no attack check re- quired.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Weaken with this modifier cannot have its ef- fects countered by another power (such as Restorative Healing) without the Persistent modifier;

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: A Weaken effect capable of reducing more than one trait at once can have this modifier, allowing you to choose which traits are affected, while not affecting others.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Combined with Precise (previously), you can use an Area Weaken to selectively af- fect only certain traits of certain targets.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: That is, you do not have the option of using the modifier or not, it always applies.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Modified Cost = base effect cost + extras - flaws FRACTIONAL COSTS If total flaws reduce an effect’s cost per rank to less than 1 power point, each additional –1 to cost per rank beyond that adds to the number of ranks of the effect you get by spending 1 power point on a 1-to-1 basis.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: If that effect has a total of –2 in modifiers, it costs 1:1, or 1 power point per rank.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: Apply- ing another –1 modifier adds to the second part of the ratio, making it 1:2, or 1 power point per two ranks, and so forth.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: A hero has Protection, which costs 1 point per rank.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: POWERS 187187 MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK ply to at least one rank, and may apply to as many ranks as the effect has.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: So the first 4 ranks of the Caliber’s Damage effect have the Burst Area modifier, costing 1 point more (or 3 per rank).

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Caliber makes a normal ranged at- tack check against the main target for his micro-rock- et launcher;

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: if he hits, the target has to resist Damage 7, and everyone within the area around the target re- sists Damage 4 (the Area Damage).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Even if he misses, the main target has to resist the Area Damage 4, since the micro-rocket explodes close by!

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: FLAT-VALUE MODIFIERS Some modifiers, rather than increasing or decreasing an ef- fect’s cost per rank, have a flat value in power points, noted as flat in the modifier’s header.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: Likewise, the Activation flaw has a flat value of –1 or –2 points, depending on how long the power takes to activate.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: If the same effect also has a flat-value extra costing 2 points and a flat-value flaw worth –1 point, then you add 2 to the final cost and sub- tract 1, for a total of (8 points for the effect + 2 points for the flat extra – 1 point for the flat flaw) or 9 power points.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: When an effect is used against a corporeal target, the effect’s rank is equal to the rank of this extra, up to a maximum of the effect’s full rank.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Characters with lower ranks 1–3 of Insubstantial do not require this extra for their effects to work on the physical world, although they can apply it to their Strength rank to allow them to exert some Strength while Insubstantial.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Rank 1 allows the effect to work at half its normal rank against insubstantial targets (rounded down);

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: If the effect Affects Only Objects, working on objects but not on living creatures, it has a net modifier of +0.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: At the GM’s discretion, someone holding, carrying, or wear- ing an object can make a Dodge resistance check against the effect, representing pulling the object out of the way at the last moment.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: AFFECTS OTHERS +0 OR +1 COST PER RANK This extra allows you to give someone else use of a per- sonal effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Both you and your subject(s) can use the effect simultaneously.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: If the effect Affects Only Others, and not you, it has a net modifier of +0.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: AFFECTS CORPOREAL FLAT • 1 POINT PER RANK An incorporeal being can use an effect with this extra on the corporeal world (see the Insubstantial effect descrip- ALTERNATE EFFECT FLAT • 1 OR 2 POINTS This modifier allows you to “swap-out” the effect for an en- tire other, alternate, effect!

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: For example, a Damage effect with the descriptor of “laser” might have a visual Dazzle as an Alternate Effect:

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: An Alternate Effect can have any rank, or combination of modifiers.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Alternate Effects may also have different de- scriptors, usually thematically linked, within reason.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: This allows you to have two versions of a Damage effect, for example:

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: POWERS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK EXTRAS NAME COST DESCRIPTION Accurate 1 flat per rank +2 attack check bonus per rank Affects Corporeal 1 flat per rank Effect works on corporeal beings with rank equal to extra rank.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Alternate Resistance +0-1 per rank Effect uses a different resistance.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: effects cannot have Alternate Effects, nor can they be Al- ternate Effects (since they can’t be turned on and off ).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: An Alternate Effect can have a total cost in power points no greater than the primary effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: So a rank 10 primary effect costing 2 points per rank, for a total of 20, can have any Alternate Effect with a cost of 20 power points or less.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: This cost does not include the cost of the Alternate Effect modifier itself.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: So if the 20-point power has 5 Alternate Ef- fects (making the final cost 25 points), each Alternate Ef- fect is still limited to a total value of 20 points (including any modifiers it may have), that of the base effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Essen- tially, each Alternate Effect has to have all of the others as Alternate Effects.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: If the advantage has any prerequisites, you must have them to gain the benefits of the advantage as a heroic feat.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: INSTANT COUNTER You can spend a hero point to attempt to counter an ef- fect used against you as a reaction.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: Among oth- er things, this option allows you to use extra effort (pre- viously) without suffering any fatigue.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Maybe one or more of the heroes have to overcome a personal problem.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The villain may have a secret the heroes need to discover, and so forth.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: A successful resistance check reduces the Area effect to half its normal rank against that target (round down, minimum of 1 rank).

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: This modifier includes the Sense-Depen- dent flaw (see Flaws) so it cannot be applied again.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: An effect must be at least close range in order to apply Area (personal range effects work only on the user by definition).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: POWERS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK As with the Feature effect, a Feature extra should be sig- nificant enough to be worth at least 1 power point and not solely based on the power’s descriptors.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: doing so is part of its “fire” descriptor and can be equally advantageous and problematic.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The Homing effect uses the same accurate sense as the original attack to “track” its target, so concealment effec- tive against that sense may confuse the effect and cause it to miss.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If a Homing attack misses due to concealment, it has lost its “lock” on the target and does not get any further chances to hit.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Any effect with a resistance difficulty modifier equal to or less than half the Impervious rank (rounded up) has no effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.object: PER RANK Effects have a standard duration
actions.raw: INCREASED DURATION +1 COST PER RANK Effects have a standard duration:

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If the effect requires an initial attack check, no ad- ditional attack check is needed to maintain it on a tar- get, but subsequent rounds of effect also do not ben- efit from critical hits.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.object: PER RANK Effects have a standard range
actions.raw: INCREASED RANGE +1 COST PER RANK Effects have a standard range:

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Increasing the range of an effect from personal to close requires either the Affects Others or Attack extras (see their descriptions).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Making a close ef- fect into a perception ranged effect requires two applica- tions of this extra, for +2 cost per rank.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: INNATE FLAT • 1 POINT An effect with this modifier is an innate part of your na- ture and unaffected by Nullify (see the Nullify effect in this chapter).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: the tar- get can perceive the use of the effect, but not its results:

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The action required to use the combined effects is the longest of its components and they use a single attack check (if one is required) and resistance check (if both effects use the same type of check).

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: If the effects have different resistances, targets check against each effect separately.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Captain Thunder has the ability to hurl thunderbolts that shock their targets with electric- ity and deafen them with powerful claps of thunder.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The combined effect costs 4 points per rank.Since both effects are ranged and require a standard action to use, so does the combined effect.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Since Damage requires a Toughness check and Affliction requires a Dodge check, the target checks against them sepa- rately, making a Toughness resistance check against the damage of the lightning and a Dodge check to avoid being deafened by the thunder.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: SINGLE TARGET To use a Multiattack against a single target, make your attack check normally.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: This circumstance bonus does not count against power level limits.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If an Impervious Resistance would ignore the attack be- fore any increase in the DC, then the attack still has no ef- fect as usual;

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: MULTIPLE TARGETS You can use Multiattack to hit multiple targets at once by “walking” or “spraying” the Multiattack across an arc.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: So making a Multiattack against five targets is a –5 penalty to each attack check.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: the target must make a resistance check against an effect rank equal to your Penetrating rank.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: So, if a rank 4 (Pene- trating 2) effect hits a target with Impervious 9, the target must resist a rank 2 effect (equal to the Penetrating rank).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: If the effect were rank 6, the target would have to resist the full effect anyway, since its rank is greater than half the Impervious rank.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: PRECISE FLAT • 1 POINT You can use a Precise effect to perform tasks requiring delicacy and fine control, such as using Precise Damage to spot-weld or carve your initials, Precise Move Object to type or pick a lock, Precise Environment to match a par- ticular temperature exactly, and so forth.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: The GM has final say as to what tasks can be performed with a Precise effect and may require an ability, skill, or power check to deter- mine the degree of precision with any given task.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.object: has a default action required to use it
actions.raw: REACTION +1 OR +3 COST PER RANK Each effect has a default action required to use it:

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Take a stan- dard action and choose an ally in your line of sight, who receives the benefits of cover against enemies in your line of sight and in range of your Multiattack.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: (You have to be able to shoot at them to get them to keep their heads down or this maneuver won’t work.) You cannot lay down a covering attack for an ally in close combat.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: For the ability to choose to have some triggering cir- cumstances activate the effect and some not, apply the Selective modifier as well.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: If Doctor Shock possessed the ability to have his aura only damage people and things he wants it to damage, he would need to have the Selec- tive modifier applied to the effect as well.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Nor- mally, you have no control over the results of such effects.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: This allows you to attack around corners, overcome cover and possibly make a surprise attack against an opponent.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The “bounce” has no effect apart from changing the attack’s direction.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: SECONDARY EFFECT +1 COST PER RANK An instant duration effect with this modifier affects the target once immediately (when the effect is used) and then once again on the following round, at the end of the attacker’s turn.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The target gets the normal resistance check against the secondary effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: So, for example, if you hit a target with a Secondary Damage Effect then, on the fol- lowing round, hit with an Affliction, the target suffers both the Affliction and the Secondary Damage.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: For a degree of selectivity with non-resistible effects, use the Precise modifier.

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: SLEEP +0 COST PER RANK When this modifier is applied to an effect that causes the incapacitated condition, the effect leaves them asleep whenever it would normally render them incapacitated.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If an attack check is required, the attacker makes one, comparing the results against each target.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Each additional rank of this modifier allows the power to split an additional time, so rank 2 allows an effect to split among three targets, then four, and so forth.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: SUSTAINED +0 COST PER RANK Applied to a permanent duration effect, this modifier makes it sustained duration, requiring a free action to use (rather than none, like other permanent effects).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The drawback is the effect requires a free action each turn to maintain it, and being unable to do so means the effect shuts off.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The Protection effect is permanent, meaning it always protects the character, but concentrating or trying harder does not make the effect more protective, nor can the character use it for power stunts.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Setting the effect requires the same ac- tion as using it normally.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: A Triggered effect is good for one use per rank in this modifier.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: A flat-value flaw cannot have more ranks than the effect itself.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: ACTIVATION FLAT • -1 OR -2 POINTS A power with this flaw requires an action to prepare or activate before any of its effects are usable.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: If the power requires a move action to activate, the flaw is –1 point.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: Activation tak- ing less than a move action is not a flaw, although may qualify as a complication (see the Power Loss complica- tion for details).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Activation has no effect other than making all of the pow- er’s effects available for use.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The effects themselves still require their normal actions to use.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: If the power is deacti- vated—either voluntarily or involuntarily via effects like Nullify—you must activate it again in order to use any of its effects.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: CHECK REQUIRED FLAT • -1 POINT PER RANK If Activation is not automatic, apply the Check Required flaw to the entire power as well and have the player make the necessary check in order to activate the power.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: If the check fails, the power does not activate, and the character has to take the activation action to try again.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: ACTIVATION AND PERMANENT EFFECTS The Activation flaw does allow permanent effects that are part of a power to be turned off, but only if the power as a whole is deactivated.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The GM should decide if allow- ing a permanent effect to have an Activation is appropri- ate based on the specific effect and any others it is com- bined with in the power.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: Stonewall has the power to turn into a super-strong rock-form.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: Stonewall’s player applies the Activation flaw to the power, saying Stonewall has to concentrate and take a standard action to assume his rock-form.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: That reduces the total cost of all three effects by 2 power points and means unless Stonewall takes a standard action to activate his rock-form, he cannot use any of the power’s effects, even including permanent ones like Protection.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: An effect with this flaw requires a check of some sort—usually a skill check—with a base difficulty of 10, +1 for each addition- al rank in Check Required.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: If the check fails, the effect doesn’t work, although the action required to use it is expended (so attempting to activate a standard action effect takes a stan- dard action whether the check is successful or not).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: If the check succeeds, the character gains the use of 1 ef- fect rank per point the check exceeds the DC.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Thus a check result of 14 allows the character to use up to 4 ranks of the effect.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: If a lesser rank of the effect doesn’t do anything, then it’s the same as failing the check.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The required check occurs as part of the action to use the effect and provides no benefit other than helping to ac- tivate it.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: POWERS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK FLAWS NAME COST DESCRIPTION Activation –1-2 flat points Effect requires a move (1 point) or standard (2 points) action to activate.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Check Required –1 flat per rank Must succeed on a check to use effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Concentration –1 per rank Sustained effect becomes concentration duration.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Effect requires a successful grab attack to use.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Increases action required to use effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Failing to use the effect causes a problematic side effect.

actions.subject: Skill
actions.raw: A spellcaster has Senses 4 (Detect Magic, Ranged, Acute, Analyze) with Expertise:

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: CHECK EXAMPLES Skill checks an effect may require include:

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Three ranks is the maximum a character can have in this flaw.

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: DISTRACTING -1 COST PER RANK Using a Distracting effect requires more concentration than usual, causing you become vulnerable when you use the effect, until the start of your next turn.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Each time you use an effect with this flaw, it loses 1 rank of effectiveness.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: For effects with a duration longer than instant, each round is considered “one use.” Once the ef- fect reaches 0 ranks, it stops working.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The GM decides when and how a faded effect recovers, but it should generally occur outside of combat and take at least an hour’s time.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If your power’s mani- festation is damaged, make a resistance check against the attack’s damage rank, using your effect’s rank as the resistance check bonus.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: For example, if a manifestation of a rank 10 effect is attacked for damage 12, you must make a resistance check against damage 12 with a +10 bonus (the effect’s rank) in place of your normal Toughness.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: GRAB-BASED -1 COST PER RANK An attack effect with this flaw requires you to successfully grab a target before using the effect (see Grab, page 248).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: This generally applies to an effect that is close range, since you have to be in close combat to grab anyway.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Lamprey has a draining touch that is a Grab-Based Weaken Strength effect.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: If his close attack check hits, the target makes a Dodge or Fortitude resistance check against Lamprey’s Strength.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: If it fails, the target then makes the Fortitude resistance check against the villain’s Weaken effect to see how much Strength Lamprey drains away.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.object: one of the following types of ac- tions
actions.raw: INCREASED ACTION -1 TO -3 COST PER RANK Using an effect requires one of the following types of ac- tions:

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: For example, an attack effect that does less damage against targets with Protection (to represent a diminished ability to penetrate armor, for example) applies the Limited flaw to only those ranks that are ineffective.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: PERMANENT -1 COST PER RANK A continuous effect with this flaw becomes permanent in duration.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: A Quirk is generally worth, at most, 2–3 power points, and many are simply 1-point flaws.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: As with Features, the GM should ensure a Quirk is truly a flaw (albeit a minor one) and not simply part of the pow- er’s descriptors.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: For example, the fact that an attack with a “sonic” descriptor likely will not travel through a vacuum is not a Quirk, simply part of the “sonic” descriptor (es- pecially since the attack may be enhanced by a medium such as water).

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: On the other hand, a shapeshifter unable to change color (losing some of the power’s utility), or a telepath unable to lie while using Mental Communication, do have Quirks to their powers.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Some effects have their range determined by rank.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: So long as the character has power points invested in the device, it can be repaired, eventually.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: It allows a Will resistance check to overcome the effect, denying you the defense bonus against that op- ponent (and applying this flaw to the effect).

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: For example, a Damage effect might involve whirling blades an attacker can avoid with a successful Dodge resistance check, circumventing the need for a Tough- ness check against the damage.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Similarly a Weaken effect based on a poison dart might add a Toughness check to see if the dart penetrates the target’s skin in addition to making the usual Fortitude check against the effect.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Otherwise the effect works normally and the target makes the usual resistance check against it, if any.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: This provides a +10 bonus to resistance checks against the effect, but gives others partial concealment from that sense.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: To give a target additional resis- tance to a sensory effect, use the Resistible flaw.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: SIDE EFFECT -1 OR -2 COST PER RANK Failing to successfully use an effect with this flaw causes some problematic effect.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Failure includes missing an at- tack check, or the target successfully resisting the effect.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Typical side effects include Affliction, Damage, or Weaken, or the base effect itself (it essentially rebounds and affects you instead).

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The Side Effect does not require an attack check and only affects you, although the GM may permit some Side Effects with the Area modi- fier on a case-by-case basis.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: TIRING -1 COST PER RANK An effect with this flaw causes you to suffer a level of fa- tigue when you use it.

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: In essence, the power requires extra effort in order to use it (see Extra Effort, page 19).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: This makes Tiring a useful flaw for creating an effect you can only use with extra effort.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: For example, a hero might have a rank 12 Damage effect, but routinely use only 8 ranks of it.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: A Tiring effect can be combined with extra effort, but the fatigue stacks, causing a minimum of two levels of fatigue per use.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: UNCONTROLLED -1 COST PER RANK You have no control over an effect with this flaw.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Roll a die each round before you use or maintain the effect.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Spending a hero point on your reliability roll al- lows you to succeed automatically (since the roll is then at least an 11).

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Alternately, instead of having a reliability roll, you can choose to have five uses where your effect works normal- ly, then it stops working altogether until you can “recover” it in some way (see the Fades flaw for more on this).

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: Powers that are only occasionally unreliable (less than about 50% of the time) are better handled as complica- tions (see Complications, page 30).

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: A gun-toting vigilante, Caliber has a variety of guns (acquired via the Equipment advantage).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Although descriptors don’t always have significant game effects in MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS, they’re perhaps the most important element in providing color and character to the powers of heroes and villains.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: Descriptors do have some affect on game play.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: For example, Immunity and Nullify work against effects with specific descriptors;

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.object: Ex- amples of origin descriptors include
actions.raw: Ex- amples of origin descriptors include:

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: Since they involve a change in the sub- ject’s DNA, mutant powers—or at least the potential for them—are inheritable.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: SOURCE A power’s source differs from its origin in that the origin is where the potential or ability to use the power comes from (where the character got the power in the first place), while source is where the power’s effect comes from, or where the power draws its energy.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: Some powers may not have or need result descriptors;

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: However, “an in- duced trance where the human brain becomes capable of accepting neurolin- guistic programming inputs” is also a valid descriptor for that same effect.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: Like medium descriptors, result descriptors may or may not match others the power al- ready has.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: it has an invented origin (someone designed and built it), a techno- logical source (it’s a technological device with a battery), uses a energy medium (an electrical shock), and results in an electrical overload of the tar- get’s nervous system (the result descriptor for its Affliction effect).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: POWERS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS CREATIVE USES OF DESCRIPTORS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK In many instances, players come up with creative uses for their characters’ descriptors.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: So if a fire-using character wants to use a tiny amount of his flame blast power to light some candles, or the electrical-controlling character wants to use some of his power output to act as a living battery to jump-start a car, go for it.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: Creative uses of descriptors with no real game effect are freebies:

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: Situations where creative uses of descriptors have a significant game effect can be handled as power stunts:

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: pick the effect that best suits the desired outcome and treat it as an Alternate Effect of the power the hero wants to use, with descrip- tors assigned as appropriate.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If an electrical-controlling hero wants to use his power like a living defibrillator to save a heart-attack victim, for example, that can be a Healing power stunt.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The hero uses extra effort (and possibly a hero point) and gets a one-shot use of Healing to stabilize the dying victim.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The GM agrees and so the heroine’s powers have no origin or source descriptors.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The key tool for handling the ap- plication of descriptors in play is the use of hero points.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: If applying a new descriptor is a setback for the hero, then award the player a hero point, just like any other setback (see Complications, page 30).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: If it’s neither, then there’s no hero point cost, just apply the descriptor.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: On the other hand, if other effects previ- ously worked on the hero as if his powers were magical, then some sort of explanation is required.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: Temporarily changing a descriptor can be a use of extra ef- fort, like any other power stunt.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: For example, a hero might change the result of an electrical Damage effect to a mag- netic Move Object effect for one use.

actions.subject: Descriptor
actions.raw: This is like any other power stunt and the changed or additional descriptors are an important part of the stunt.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: GADGETS & GEAR MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS EQUIPMENT DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK In addition to their amazing devices, characters often make use of various mundane equipment—ordinary things found in the real world—ranging from a simple set of tools to cell phones, laptop computers, and even common appliances.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Each piece of equipment has a cost in points, just like other traits.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: The character pays the item’s cost out of the points from the Equipment advantage and can thereafter have and use that item.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: EQUIPMENT EFFECTS AND FEATURES An item’s cost is based on its effects and features, just like a power (see the Powers chapter for more informa- tion), so a ranged weapon has a cost based on its Ranged Damage rank.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Equipment can have the Alternate Effect modifier (see the Extras section of the Powers chapter), such as a weapon capable of different modes of opera- tion, or a reconfigurable tool.

actions.subject: Array
actions.raw: Characters can also have Alternate Equipment, an array of items usable only one at a time.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Alternate Equipment can also include things like an arsenal of weapons the char- acter can swap out, providing different sets of weapons, with only a limited number usable at once.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The GM may allow players to spend a hero point in order to have a particular item of equip- ment “on-hand” at a particular time.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: This is essentially an equipment “power stunt”—a one-time use of the item for one scene—and the Gamemaster rules whether or not having a particular item on-hand is even possible.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: For example, a hero out for an evening in his secret identity might have something like a concealed weapon or other small item on-hand, but it’s unlikely the character is carry- ing a large weapon or item unless he has some means of concealing it.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: RESTRICTED EQUIPMENT The Gamemaster may rule some equipment is simply not available or that characters must pay for an additional Feature (or more) in order to have it.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: This may include cer- tain kinds of weapons, vehicles, and anything else the GM feels should be restricted in the series.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: REPAIRING AND REPLACING Repairing an item requires a Technology check.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: AMMO, BATTERIES, AND CHARGES Lots of equipment has a limited lifespan:

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: However, it can be a hassle to keep track of the lifespan of every piece of equipment the heroes may have (to say nothing of all the villains and supporting characters).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The material in this book assumes equipment and devices have effectively unlimited use, except when the GM declares a complication, and that heroes properly maintain, charge, reload, and refuel their gear “off-panel” in between the scenes of an adventure, unless the Gamemaster dictates otherwise.

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: Steel cuffs have Toughness 9 and are DC 20 to escape using Sleight of Hand (or Technology to pick the lock).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: SURVEILLANCE GEAR Heroes often use surveillance gear to keep tabs on crimi- nals and their activities.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: It requires an Investigation or Perception check against the results of the Sleight of Hand check used to conceal the microphone to find it.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: It requires a successful attack check to plant a tracer on a target (or a Sleight of Hand check to plant it outside of action time).

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: Night vision goggles use passive light gathering to improve vision in near-dark conditions.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: These tinted eye-coverings provide pro- tection against blinding light, granting a +5 equipment bonus to resistance checks against visual Dazzle attacks involving bright light.

actions.subject: Device
actions.raw: This device contains several different screw- drivers, a knife blade or two, can opener, bottle opener, file, short ruler, scissors, tweezers, and wire cutters.

actions.subject: Array
actions.raw: Some characters may have a Removable array of devices instead, allowing for far more unusual ef- fects than run-of-the-mill equipment.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Still, heroes often have 1-point items in their utility belts, like flashlights, re- breathers, and so forth.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The tear gas, as the most expensive effect, has full cost.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Characters who don’t have any offen- sive powers often rely on weapons to get the job done.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: They typically have a Strength-based Damage effect (see the Damage effect in the Powers chapter), adding the wielder’s Strength rank to the weapon’s damage rank.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: If a wielder exerts Strength greater than the weapon’s Toughness (4 for wooden weapons, 7 or 8 for metal weapons), the weapon breaks when it is used.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Melee weapons have the following traits:

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The effect a hit with the weapon causes, typically Damage, although some modern melee weapons have other effects.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: The effect has the normal cost given in the Powers chapter.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: The effect may also have certain descrip- tors, such as bludgeoning or slashing, for defining things like resistance or vulnerability to certain effects.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Some weapons have a larger threat range than others.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Characters pay this cost from their equipment points to have a weapon of this type as part of their regular equipment.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: GADGETS & GEAR 217217 MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK RANGED WEAPONS Ranged weapons include both thrown and projectile weapons.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Projec- tile weapons include bows, crossbows, and guns as well as energy weapons like lasers and blasters.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Like melee weapons, ranged weapons have category, ef- fect, critical, and cost traits.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: A shotgun can fire shot, which does Damage 5 with Accurate 1 due to the spread, but Limited to Dam- age 3 against targets with Protection.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: Rifles designed for long-range use, typically in conjunction with a powerful scope or targeting system.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: A bow-wielding character may have various “trick” ar- rows with different powers, typically handled as devices.

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: A set of weighted cords intended to entangle an opponent with a Snare Affliction that hinders and im- pedes, then renders the target defenseless and immobile.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Boomerang wielders often use this property of the weapon to feint, allowing for an at- tack against a vulnerable target on the return arc on the attacker’s next turn.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: SUPER-SHIELDS Just as power armor is a device version of otherwise or- dinary equipment armor, some heroes (and, less often, villains) have shield devices providing them with great- er benefits than an ordinary shield.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: This is best handled as an Alternate Effect of the shield, mean- ing you can’t use it both offensively and defensively at the same time!

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: A hero able to hurl a shield at foes can even have a Ranged Damage effect with it.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Sometimes heroes make use of other means to get around.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: VEHICLE TRAITS Vehicles have the following traits:

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Vehicles can even have power effects of their own.

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: These include seating, safety harnesses or seat belts, heating and air-conditioning, radio receiver, headlights, and similar things with little or no impact on game play.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: A vehicle can have power effects of its own, usually re- flecting the vehicle’s systems.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Vehicle powers have their normal cost for the ve- hicle (meaning they cost one-fifth the normal amount for the vehicle’s owner, since the effects are incorporated into the vehicle and cost equipment points rather than power points).

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Some vehicles may have Sustained Protection (such as force screens) instead of, or in addi- tion to, Permanent Protection.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Some vehicles may also have Concealment from auditory senses or things such as radar, giving them a “stealth mode.” 4 points (normal vision or all of another sense type) or 8 points (all visual senses).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: ALTERNATE VEHICLES Just like Alternate Equipment, characters may have mul- tiple vehicles.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The hero’s player can even spend a hero point to pull out a motorcycle, submarine, jet-ski, or whatever other vehicle the hero might have stashed away waiting for the right occasion.

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: GROUND VEHICLES Most cars include such standard features as air condition- ing, air bags, antilock brakes, cruise control, keyless entry, and an AM/FM radio with CD player.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Luxury vehicles often also include extras such as power seats, leather uphol- stery, and a sunroof.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS Trucks include pick-ups, sport utility vehicles (SUVs), vans, and similarly sized vehicles.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The standard tank has Tough- Impervious ness 12 and comes equipped with a can- non (Ranged Damage 10, Burst Area 6) and a heavy machine gun (Ranged Multiattack Damage 6).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Battleships have massive gun batteries (Ranged Damage 13, Burst Area 9) and heavy armor.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Fighter jets have machine guns (Ranged Multiattack Damage 6) and air-to-air missiles (Ranged Damage 11, Burst Area 8, Homing 6).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Bombers may have machine guns and missiles, but also have powerful bombs (Burst Area Damage 12 or higher) they can drop on targets.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK SPACE VEHICLES Space vehicles are intended for use outside the atmo- sphere, some of them for interplanetary or even interstel- lar travel.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Generally space vehicles are found in the pos- session of alien civilizations, although the GM may choose to allow some organizations and individuals on Earth to have space vehicles.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Space cruisers have larger beam weapons (Ranged Dam- age 12) and often energy torpedoes (Ranged Damage 12, Burst Area 10, Homing 8).

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Space battleships have the most massive weapons:

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Teams may even pool their equipment points to have a headquarters they share, with the Gamemaster’s approval.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: A character can even have multiple bases of operation (see Alternate Headquarters later in this section).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: This is more common for villains, who have back-up plans and secret bases they can retreat to when their plans are defeated.

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: Each increase in size category costs 1 point, each decrease in size category gives you an additional point to spend elsewhere on your HEADQUARTERS TRAIT COST TRAIT STARTING VALUE EQUIPMENT COST Size Small Toughness Features 6 — 1 point per size category 1 point per +2 Toughness 1 point per feature STRUCTURE SIZE CATEGORIES STRUCTURE SIZE COST EXAMPLES Awesome Colossal Gargantuan Huge Large Medium Small Tiny Diminutive Fine Miniscule 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 –1 –2 –3 –4 Small town, sprawling installation City block, private estate Skyscraper Castle Mansion, cave complex Warehouse House Townhouse Apartment Loft Room headquarters, although you’re not going to have a lot of room for extras!

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Gen- erally, a combat simulator has a suite of equipment that can simulate any appropriate attack effect at a rank up to the HQ power level.

actions.subject: Skill
actions.raw: The system’s access to restricted communication bands depends on the clearance and skills of the user.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Heroes often have access to special government channels, while a successful Technology skill check (DC 25) can grant a user illegal access to re- stricted systems.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: This allows characters to make full use of the Technology skill and the computer can be pro- grammed to handle routine base functions (including monitoring communications channels and controlling defensive systems).

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Skill checks to locate the headquarters have their DC increased by +10.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: A defense system can have any attack effect with a cost no greater than twice the HQ power level.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Their attack bonus is equal to the power level.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Some may be intended to merely incapacitate and capture intruders (more along the lines of a nonlethal Defense System), allowing the villain to interrogate them...and then perhaps put them into a real deathtrap!

actions.subject: Modifier
actions.raw: Headquarters that have no “exterior” structure, such as an extra-dimensional fortress accessed by a magical talisman, do not have this feature, but instead have things like Dimensional Portal, Isolated, Sealed, and the like.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: The ef- fect cannot have a total cost greater than twice the HQ power level and cannot exceed the power level limits.

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: If the headquarters has features like Defense System and Security System, they also extend over the grounds (with fences, sensors, weapon emplacements, and so forth).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Heroes use holding cells to contain cap- tured villains until they can be turned over to the proper authorities while villains use them to keep heroes pris- DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK oner until they can dispose of them in their latest death- trap.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: INFIRMARY An infirmary consists of hospital beds and equipment for the full use of the Treatment skill.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: An infirmary can provide treatment for a number of characters equal to the base’s power level at one time and it can be assumed to have the necessary facilities to handle any unusual physiology of the base’s owner(s).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The base’s owner doesn’t have to worry about things like door-to-door salesmen or other unwanted visitors but the headquarters is also far from civilization (which can be limiting for heroes unable to travel fast).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Characters can use the laboratory to perform research, study unusual phenomena (including many super-powers), and so forth.

actions.subject: Skill
actions.raw: LIBRARY A library allows for use of various Knowledge skills when doing research.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: This is usually a number of residents equal to the HQ’s power level comfortably (pos- sibly more, at the GM’s discretion).

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: The headquarters also has emergency back-up power should the generators fail.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: This generally lasts for a number of hours equal to the HQ’s power level.

actions.subject: Equipment
actions.raw: Given the equip- ment point costs of most HQs, team members rarely have to devote more than a single rank of the Equipment ad- vantage to their headquarters, an any excess points may also cover things like team vehicles and equipment (com- mlinks, for example).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: In the event that a character has more than one headquar- ters, such as hidden bases scattered around the world, the others are treated as Alternate Equipment:

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The extra headquarters are largely a convenience (for heroes visiting other areas) and insurance against the loss of any one HQ.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: GADGETS & GEAR MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK SAMPLE HEADQUARTERS SEA-BASE Gamemasters can use the following sample headquarters as ready-made lairs for supervillains while players can use them as bases for their heroes.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Constructs are subject to the same power level limits as other characters and the Gamemaster should require con- structs controlled by the players to observe these limits.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Non-player character constructs have their power level determined the same as other NPCs.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: ABILITIES Constructs have no Stamina, because they are not living beings.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Constructs also have either no Intel- lect and Presence ranks or no Strength and Agility ranks.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Like the comics, MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS is about action, so this chapter looks at the flow of the game and how heroes accomplish their amazing feats, ranging from last-minute rescues and brilliant investigations to thrilling battles against the forces of evil.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: The chapter starts out with a look at action rounds, used to measure time when seconds count, then moves on to the different challenges heroes face, followed by handling conflicts like super-powered battles.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Base initiative bonus is equal to the character’s Agility rank.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Many characters have ad- vantages or powers that modify their initiative, such as Improved Initiative.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: If two characters have the same initiative result, they act in order of highest Dodge bo- nus first, then highest Agility and highest Awareness.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: SKILLS Choose the skill ranks you want your character to have and pay 1 power point per 2 total skill ranks.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: ADVANTAGES Choose the advantages you want your character to have and pay 1 power point per advantage or rank in a advan- tage.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: COMPLICATIONS Choose at least two complications for your hero.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Once your GM has ap- proved, your new hero is ready for play!

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Each ability, skill, advantage, power, and other trait has an associated power point cost.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: Once spent, power points cannot be re-allo- cated without the use of a particular power or the Game- master’s permission.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: NO ACTION Finally, some things players are called upon to do—cer- tain die rolls like resistance checks, for example—are not considered actions at all, as they involve no action on the part of the characters.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: ACTION & ADVENTURE MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK though you can move (move action) and then attack (stan- dard action) or attack and then move, you cannot move half your distance, attack, and then move the other half unless you have some special trait that allows you to do so.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: • Make any necessary resistance checks to recover You can use extra effort in order to take an additional standard or move action on your turn (see Extra Effort on page 19).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Some challenges are quick and involve only a single character, such as a hero making a daring leap or acrobatic maneuver, while others are more involved and require the efforts of a whole team, such as clearing all of the people out of a burning building or search- ing the entire city (or world!) for an escaped criminal.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: If all of the characters get a turn and it does not particu- larly matter who goes first, then the Gamemaster can dis- pense with initiative for the challenge.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: For example, if the heroes all have to leap across a chasm, then it is a chal- lenge they must all complete, and it does not particularly matter which of them goes first or last in doing so (since their actions are all virtually simultaneous).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: So, for example, if the GM determines that part of a burning building will collapse after the first round, initiative may be checked to see which heroes go before the collapse and who does not quite act fast enough.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: It can involve several checks of the same trait, or multiple traits, such as a Persuasion check to get the heroes on good terms with the King of Atlantis, an Expertise check to explain the particular dan- ger the kingdom faces, and an Insight check to realize the king’s advisor has something other than the best interests of the kingdom and royal family at heart.

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: Other consequences might include par- ticular conditions (see Conditions in The Basics chapter);

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: Sometimes the environment itself can be a danger, particularly when villains try to use it to their advantage.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Fatigued characters who fail a check become exhausted, then incapacitated, at which point the charac- ter’s condition becomes dying after another failed Forti- tude check.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: How often characters have to make Fortitude checks de- pends on the conditions.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: SUFFOCATION Characters can hold their breath for ten rounds (one min- ute) plus a number of rounds equal to twice their Stamina.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Failure on the Fortitude check means the character becomes incapaci- tated .

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Falling into or onto a dangerous surface may cause additional damage, at the GM’s discretion.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Catching a falling person or object requires a Dexterity check (DC 5).

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Poisons generally have one of several effects particularly Afflic- tion, Damage, or Weaken.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Some poisons may even have multiple Linked effects.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: A Treatment skill check can provide a bonus on resistance checks against poison.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.raw: Some diseases may have multiple linked effects.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: A Treatment skill check can provide a bonus on resistance checks against disease.

actions.subject: Power
actions.raw: RADIATION Radiation in the comic books often causes mutations or triggers latent powers in those exposed to it rather than simply causing radiation sickness.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The victim makes an initial Fortitude check against (DC 10 + radiation’s rank) and an additional check each day.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Radiation sickness is typically a Weaken Stamina effect, but may include other effects, includ- 238238

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: Affliction conditions typically include impaired and disabled, perhaps also dazed and stunned for especially severe nausea.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Common Affliction conditions include impaired and disabled, although a large enough dose or repeated exposure can also Weaken Stamina, even leading to death.

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Typically a Weaken effect against Strength, Agility, or Stamina (sometimes more than one), with Weaken Stamina potentially lethal, if the victim’s Stamina drops below –5.

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: Side-effects might include conditions like dazed, impaired, or hindered.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: At the GM’s discre- tion, radiation exposure can lead to other effects, such as damage to a hero’s power ranks (causing a temporary decrease in powers).

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Heroes able to ignore the effects of deep space must have Immunity to suffocation, vacuum, and radiation, at a mini- mum.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Conflict between heroes and villains is a prime part of MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS and a big element of the fun, just like the colorful and spec- tacular fights in the superhero comic books.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If your result equals or exceeds the target’s defense, your attack hits and may have some effect.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: To find out if it’s a critical hit, determine if the attack check total is equal to or greater than the target’s defense.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: INCREASED THREAT RANGE Characters with the Improved Critical advantage (see page 136) can score a threat on a natural result less than 20, although they still automatically hit only on a natural 20.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Any attack check that doesn’t result in a hit is not a threat.

actions.subject: Effect
actions.object: when the critical hit is determined
actions.raw: A critical hit can have one of the following three effects, chosen by the player when the critical hit is determined:

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: Against a min- ion, this bypasses the resistance check entirely;

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The target makes resis- tance checks against the attack’s initial and added effects separately.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The critical hit results in an alter- nate effect for the attack, like a use of extra effort for a power stunt (see Extra Effort in The Basics chap- ter), except the character suffers no fatigue as a re- sult.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: ACTION & ADVENTURE DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DAMAGE A successful attack with a Damage effect requires the tar- get to make a Toughness resistance check.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The target has a –1 circumstance penalty to further resistance checks against damage.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The target is dazed until the end of their next turn and has a –1 circumstance penalty to further checks against damage.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The target is staggered and has a -1 circumstance penalty to further checks against damage.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The circumstance penalties to Toughness checks are cu- mulative, so a target who fails three resistance checks against Damage, each with one degree of failure, has a total –3 penalty.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: If an incapacitated target fails a resistance check against Damage, the target’s condition shifts to dying.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: A dying target who fails a resistance check against Damage is dead.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: The GM rolls attack checks against the heroes.

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: Two smugglers shoot at Ultramarine and three shoot at Princess, but they both have Impervious Toughness 8.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Then, suddenly… The GM rolls an attack check against Princess, getting a 15 result.

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: I don’t think my weapons will do much against Trawler’s armor.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Compares Rook’s attack against DC 10, a definite hit and an automatic critical for +5 damage.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The GM checks the net’s Toughness of 9 against DC 25 (15 + 5 for the talons + 5 for the critical), generating a 23 result.

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: Trawler has Toughness 10 and the GM rolls a 9.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Trawler staggers back, clearly dazed by the blast… The heroes have Trawler and her smugglers on the ropes, but will Princess get free in time to help?

actions.subject: Condition
actions.raw: Dazed and staggered results have no real effect on inanimate targets, since they do not take actions.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If an attacker’s intention is to bend, break or destroy an ob- ject, then two degrees of failure on the Toughness check results in a break (such as a hole punched through the object) while three or more degrees of failure means the object is destroyed (shattered, smashed to pieces, etc.).

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: Equipment has Toughness based on its material.

actions.subject: Defense
actions.raw: Devices have a base Toughness equal to the total points in the device divided by 5 (rounded down, minimum of 1).

actions.subject: Ability
actions.raw: Objects, having no Stamina, do not recover from damage unless they have an effect like Regeneration.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: RANGE An attack has one of three ranges:

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: At medium range (up to rank x 50 feet), the attack check has a –2 circumstance modi- fier.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: At long range (up to rank x 100 feet), the attack check has a –5 circumstance modifier.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: MATERIAL TOUGHNESS PERCEPTION AND AREA EFFECTS Paper Soil Glass Ice Rope Wood Stone Iron Reinforced Concrete Steel Titanium Super-alloys RECOVERY 0 0 1 1 1 3 5 7 8 9 15 20+ Perception and Area effects do not require attack checks, they automatically affect a given target or area (see the Area extra in the Powers chapter).

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: CONCEALMENT To attack a target, you first have to have some idea of where to aim your attack.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: So a damaged character recovers from being inca- pacitated, then staggered, dazed, and finally removes a –1 Toughness check penalty per minute until fully recovered.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: ACTION & ADVENTURE MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK NO COVER PARTIAL COVER TOTAL COVER COVER Targets may also hide behind obstructions to gain cover against your attacks.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: • Certain traits (like the Takedown advantage) are more effective against or specifically target minions.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: Defense Class = defense + 10 Cover also grants a circumstance bonus to Dodge resis- tance checks against area effects equal to its penalty to attack checks, so long as the target has cover with respect to the origin point of the effect.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: So someone behind total cover also gains a +5 to Dodge checks against area effects.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Villains often employ hordes of minions against heroes.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: • • Non-minions can make attack checks against min- ions as routine checks.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: So a minion failing a So a hero with Parry 11 has a defense class of 21 (11 + 10) against close attacks.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: If the same hero has Dodge 9, that is a defense class of 19 (9 + 10) against ranged attacks.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: So the aforementioned hero with Parry 11 and Dodge 9 would have ranks of Parry 6 and Dodge 5 while vulnerable.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: What’s more, attackers can make attack checks against defenseless targets as routine checks (see Routine Checks in The Basics chapter), meaning a hit is guaranteed with an attack bonus of 0 or more, unless there are other modifiers affecting the check.

actions.subject: Attack
actions.raw: ACTION & ADVENTURE 245245 MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS RESISTANCE A successful attack has some effect on the target.

actions.subject: Check
actions.raw: The effect has a rank, used to determine a difficulty class for the target’s resistance check.

actions.raw: If the DC is low enough that anyone can succeed as a routine check, then it may be too low, or the action may not be worth assigning a check.

actions.subject: Character
actions.raw: Of course, If the situation is stressful—such as the midst of combat—then a routine check is not an option, and a lower DC can provide heroes with a reasonably high chance of success with just a small chance of failure for dramatic purposes.

actions.subject: Advantage
actions.raw: “What happens?” The player might say, “I follow a couple quick jabs with a powerful uppercut that sends him stumbling back against the railing of the catwalk, then I press my advantage, so we’re both struggling up against it, high above the factory floor.” Alternately, the player might say, “He stumbles back a few steps and I take the opportunity to draw a concealed bolo from my utility belt, which I’m going to use to tie him up next turn.” Same game system outcome, very different descriptions.

actions.subject: Character

actions.subject: Ability

actions.subject: Character
