Unexpected twists and turns are part of the fun of an RPG, not only for the players, but also for you when you run the game.

“What happens?” The player might say, “I follow a couple quick jabs with a powerful uppercut that sends him stumbling back against the railing of the catwalk, then I press my advantage, so we’re both struggling up against it, high above the factory floor.” Alternately, the player might say, “He stumbles back a few steps and I take the opportunity to draw a concealed bolo from my utility belt, which I’m going to use to tie him up next turn.” Same game system outcome, very different descriptions.
part of the fun of the game is for the heroes to deal with setbacks and challenges, and the players will often provide you with ideas in their descriptions.
Maybe a hero’s near miss damages some part of the scenery, or endangers innocent bystanders.

Besides, the complication system of MUTANTS & MASTER- MINDS provides you with the perfect excuse to “cheat” to help out the heroes’ adversaries from time to time, and to compensate the players in the process by awarding them hero points, which they, in turn, can use to “cheat” the fickle die from time to time and ensure their heroes succeed.
So when all else fails, just have a player make a check with the most appropriate trait (ability, skill, or power).
Likewise, if a power level 10 hero is going to take out a PL 3 thug, you don’t have to make all the rolls.
RANK Every trait in MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS —abilities, skills, pow- ers, and so forth—has an associated rank, a value telling you how strong (or weak) that trait is.
DIFFICULTY CLASS Every task—from making an attack to avoiding harm to figuring out a gadget—has a Difficulty Class or DC, a value that tells you how hard that task is to perform.
This is true whether you’re trying to use a skill, make an attack, use a power, or what have you.

When in doubt, or whenever you want to speed the game along, just have a player make a check of the appro- priate trait rank against a DC based on how difficult the task is and you can’t really go wrong.

MAINTAINING GAME BALANCE Part of the Gamemaster’s job is to make sure the game is fair and balanced, so everyone can have a good time and all the heroes have an equal chance of doing some 255255 MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK fun and exciting things in the course of the adventure.
If you plan to have mystery stories, you may want to consider limiting Mind Reading, Postcognition, and similar effects, or finding innovative ways for villains to get around them.
Note that the GM has the authority to say “no” to a particu- lar power effect or other trait, even if it is perfectly “legal” in terms of the game rules and the power level of the se- ries.
how to tell them a power they want is unbalanced, how to tell them the character they have in mind doesn’t fit into the group, and so forth.

True, sometimes you must be firm and say “no” to something for the bet- terment of the game and to safeguard everyone’s enjoy- ment, but a Gamemaster who also learns to say “yes” to the players can ensure everyone has fun.
It may be a particularly innovative maneuver, a new use for a skill or power, using the environment to their advantage in some way, or something you never would have consid- ered before.
This works particularly well with innovative uses of powers.
If a player wants to do something that isn’t normally a part of the char- acter’s abilities, require a hero point to make the at- tempt.

The hero point doesn’t do anything but let the character try something outlandish, and play- ers won’t be able to pull off such stunts all the time because they have a limited number of hero points to spend.
Tell your players you made a mistake letting that power into the game in the first place and you have to change the way it works in order to make the game fun and fair for everyone.
Use minion characters and have a “taken out” result against them equal the minion being killed rather than simply incapacitated.
Have certain kinds of attacks—such as guns, fire, or lasers—always count as “going for the kill” when they are used.
Have four or more degrees of failure on a resistance check against Damage (and Afflictions that cause the dying condition) result in immediate death.
Essen- tially, the more the heroes struggle early on in the game, the more resources (in this case, hero points) they have to overcome the villain later .
This may come from other RPGs, where defeat has much more serious conse- quences, up to and including the death of the heroes!

Point out that an early defeat by the villain is not neces- sarily a “loss,” but a complication, and that they earn hero points for complications, leading up to the point where they can use their earned points against the villain.

When you do have the heroes defeated as a complication, make sure the players all know that there is no chance for their he- roes to avoid this once you spring it on them, to minimize the opportunity for them to struggle and rail hopelessly against it.
In a game, you may have to contend with players unwilling to let the villain go, so long as there’s any chance of snatch- ing victory from the jaws of defeat.
GUEST HEROES Comic books sometimes have “guest stars” in them and some guest heroes are more popular than the main char- acters!
That’s not to say you can’t have guest heroes show up in your adventures.
For example, if you have a story wherein mind-controlled heroes try to upstage the player characters, then go right ahead and have the NPCs steal their thunder.
Have them be the ones defeated and captured by the villains, for example, and allow the player charac- ters to rescue them (also dealing with the issue of cap- tures, previously).
Have NPCs come to the heroes for aid or advice.

For example, you can allow the players to run members of another hero team, meeting their regular characters as NPCs, or you can have two teams band together, mixing and matching characters from each and having each player run a regular hero and a guest hero.
This can be a great way to include various heroes as guest characters in your games.
LETHALITY Comics have different levels of lethality and character death.
That’s all well and good in a solo story, but difficult to include in a team of superheroes.
Then sketch out the overall plot, and describe the encounters the heroes are likely to have during the adven- ture.
You’ll also want to make sure you have statistics and character sheets for important supporting characters and vil- lains.
If this is your first adventure, you should also decide if the heroes are already together as a team or if they will have the opportunity to form a team during the adventure.
Have your last few adventures all been crime-fighting?
260260 ENCOUNTERS Your plot outline gives you a number of potential en- counters the heroes may have during the adventure.
This means the early encounters in the adventure don’t have to go well for the heroes.
The more setbacks the heroes face early on, the more hero points they earn for use later in the adventure.
This is when the heroes confront the major threat of the adventure and have a real chance to deal with it.

The city is safe once again.” AWARDS While adventure and action might be a hero’s sole re- ward, RPG players prefer something a bit more tangible they can use to “keep score.” Even if there are no winners or losers in an RPG per se, there are still ways to reward
AWARDING HERO POINTS Part of the Gamemaster’s job is handing out hero points to the players, which influences the flow of the adven- ture and helps the heroes accomplish important tasks later on.
Essentially, you decide how easy or difficult a time the heroes have by how many hero points you hand out, and by how many complications you make them face to earn those points.
As GM, you have discretionary power over hero points.
This represents the experience and confi- dence the heroes have gained, along with other factors con- tributing to an increase in their abilities, skills, and powers.

The Gamemaster also may choose not to award a power point for an adventure in which the heroes did especially poorly, such as failing to defeat a vil- lain’s major scheme or allowing many innocent people to suffer harm they could have prevented.

When they have earned another 15 power points (bringing their total up to 165), the GM should consider raising the series power level to 11, allowing the heroes to spend some of those points to increase traits currently at the maximum limit.

It’s also fine to have others lag behind, as the heroes outstrip some of their old foes, who no longer represent the kind of threat they did before, plus you can always introduce new villains and challenges suited to the series power level as things progress.
Some very experienced heroes may be as high as PL15, but have power point to- tals well in excess of the recommended starting amount for that level.
They’re good examples of how heroes can develop in a series where the power level has become “fixed” but the GM still provides power point awards for advancement.
Heroes often use these rewards to help fund their own efforts (paying for their equipment, headquarters, etc.) or else donate it to charity.
Other heroes are more mer- cenary and may even require a paying client before they’ll use their powers to help anyone!

The authorities may want to impound super-devices as evidence or to have them examined by “top men,” but they may allow heroes to keep them in some cases (particularly if the heroes don’t tell anyone about them...).
Generally any device a player intends to become a regular part of a hero’s abilities should be paid for using earned power points.
The GM may require the hero to have enough power points set aside for the device or that all of the hero’s future earned power points go toward “paying off” the device’s cost.
One-time use of a captured device may be allowed by having the player spend a hero point, similar to a power stunt or invention.
Again, the GM decides what things require power point expenditure and which are free—at least in terms of point cost.
Comic books have told stories about heroes from nearly every time period.
Heroes can stalk the streets of dark, towering cities trying to protect the innocent where a corrupt legal system has failed.
The dark style allows players considerable freedom, since the heroes could, and often do, use any means available to accomplish their goals.
they give the Gamemaster a “handle” on your hero, different challenges to introduce or include in adventures.
When the GM does so, you earn hero points you can use to enhance your character’s chances of success, amongst other things.
Keep in mind the adventure needs to have room for all of the heroes’ complications, so individual ones can only come up so often.
MOTIVATION Every hero has something that drove him or her to be- come a hero in the first place—a motivation that keeps them going when things get tough.
The GM can use your hero’s motivation to encourage certain actions, and enemies may do the same.
Common heroic motivations include the following:
Some such heroes see their powers as more of a curse than a blessing, but try to do some good with them while hoping and looking for a way to have a normal life.
The responsibility of having great power can be a heavy burden but some heroes feel it is their duty to use the powers they’ve been given for the greater good.
exceptional abilities and have decided, either independently, or as a group, to become heroes!
Does your two-fisted hero have the guts to pass up the glory of answering the President’s call to serve a higher cause?

In the wake of multiversal threats, including the machi- nations of powerful extradimensional beings, a mysteri- ous organization has instituted a program of recruiting agents from across the various worlds.
Abilities 28 + Powers 0 + Advantages 3 + Skills 22 + Defenses 8 = 61 Government agents include members of organizations like the FBI, CIA, DEA, ATF, and even the DEO.
and plainclothes officers have more ranks in Investigate and often in other social skills like Persuasion and Intimidation.

Abilities 28 + Powers 0 + Advantages 6 + Skills 18 + Defenses 8 = 60 SWAT (Special Weapons And Tactics) squads are made up of police officers with special training in squad-level tactics and weapon-use.
Specialists and officers have appropriate addition- al training (and skills).
COMPLICATIONS AND UP-FRONT REWARDS Some roleplaying game systems include complications, disadvantages, or similar problematic character traits which offer “bonus points” for creating the character;

Plus, after they have “paid out” their initial benefit, front-loaded negative traits are nothing but a burden to the character from that point forward, leading players to try and avoid or mitigate them as much as possible.
This means that although the hero has to deal with some “bad stuff” from time to time, there is an upside, and a reason for players to want their characters’ complications to come into play!
For example, a hero might have the quirk of always leaving some sort of “calling card” for the authorities along with a captured criminal.
That could become a complication if somebody else starts imitating it, or uses it to cause trouble for the hero.
Afflic- tion (see the Powers chapter) is the typical effect, but some weaknesses inflict outright Damage, Weaken the target, or have some other effect.
As mentioned previous- ly, the Gamemaster may set limits as to how many ongoing complications your hero can have in play at any given time.
For example, while the game rules don’t have specific details for how to handle a hero with a concussion, it can make for an interesting story.
While heroes tend to share a desire to use their powers for good and uphold the law, they also show a diverse range of attitudes.
This represents the experi- ence and confidence the heroes have gained, along with other factors contributing to an improvement in their abilities, skills, and powers.

The Gamemaster also may choose not to award a power point for an adventure in which the heroes did especially poorly, such as failing to defeat a villain’s major scheme or al- lowing many innocent people to suffer harm they could have prevented.
So if your hero has a power costing 2 points per rank, and you want to improve it by 1 rank, spend 2 of your earned power points to do so.

Just like starting power points, once earned power points have been allocated to a trait, they remain that way, un- less some effect (such as a transformed condition) causes the character’s point allocation to change.

When they have earned another 15 power points (bringing their to- tal up to 165), the GM should consider raising the power level to 11, allowing the heroes to spend some of those power points to increase traits which are currently at the maximum limit.

It’s also fine to have others lag be- hind, as the heroes outstrip some of their old foes, who no longer represent the kind of threat they did before, plus you can introduce new villains and challenges suited to the series power level as things progress.

It’s possible, however, that you have an advantage that doesn’t mesh correctly with your skills.
For example, you might get the Daze (Intimidation), Skill Mastery (Technol- ogy), or Assessment advantages, each of which requires a specific skill to work correctly:

Usually, this process is as simple as replacing ranks of one effect for equivalent ranks of another ef- fect, but remember, if you choose to delete a rank or two from Presence and use those points to buy some ranks of Stealth or to increase your Dodge defense, for example, you’ll need to make sure you adjust any skill totals that changed because of the hero’s lowered Presence rank.

modifiers.raw: ULTIMATE EFFORT FORTUNE You can spend a hero point on a particular check and treat the roll as a 20 (meaning you don’t need to roll the die at all, just apply a result of 20 to your modifier).

modifiers.description: Power Cost = ((base effect costs + extras - flaws) x rank) + flat modifiers POWER DESCRIPTORS The rules in this chapter explain what the various powers do, that is, what their game effects are, but it is left up to the play- er and Gamemaster to apply descriptors to define exactly what a power is and what it looks (and sounds, and feels) like to observers beyond just a collection of game effects.
modifiers.raw: Power Cost = ((base effect costs + extras - flaws) x rank) + flat modifiers POWER DESCRIPTORS The rules in this chapter explain what the various powers do, that is, what their game effects are, but it is left up to the play- er and Gamemaster to apply descriptors to define exactly what a power is and what it looks (and sounds, and feels) like to observers beyond just a collection of game effects.

modifiers.description: A power’s descriptors are primarily for color.
modifiers.raw: A power’s descriptors are primarily for color.

modifiers.description: It’s more inter- esting and clear to say a hero has a “Flame Blast” or “Light- ning Bolt” power than a generic “Damage effect.” “Flame” and “lightning” are descriptors for the Damage effect.
modifiers.raw: It’s more inter- esting and clear to say a hero has a “Flame Blast” or “Light- ning Bolt” power than a generic “Damage effect.” “Flame” and “lightning” are descriptors for the Damage effect.

modifiers.description: De- scriptors do have some impact on the game since some ef- fects work only on or with effects of a particular descriptor.
modifiers.raw: De- scriptors do have some impact on the game since some ef- fects work only on or with effects of a particular descriptor.

modifiers.description: A hero may be immune to fire and heat, for example, so any effect with the “fire” or “heat” descriptor doesn’t affect that character.
modifiers.raw: A hero may be immune to fire and heat, for example, so any effect with the “fire” or “heat” descriptor doesn’t affect that character.

modifiers.description: Generally speaking, a descriptor is part of what a power is called beyond its game system name.
modifiers.raw: Generally speaking, a descriptor is part of what a power is called beyond its game system name.

modifiers.description: Her Damage effect is the power to throw lightning bolts, so it has the descriptor “lightning.” If a villain can absorb electricity, then his power works against the heroine’s Damage (since lightning is electrical in nature).
modifiers.raw: Her Damage effect is the power to throw lightning bolts, so it has the descriptor “lightning.” If a villain can absorb electricity, then his power works against the heroine’s Damage (since lightning is electrical in nature).

modifiers.description: Concealment creates thick banks of fog, giving it the “fog” or “mist” descriptor.
modifiers.raw: Concealment creates thick banks of fog, giving it the “fog” or “mist” descriptor.

modifiers.description: Her Environment is the power to control the weather, giving it the descriptor “weather.” If the hero- ine’s power comes as a gift from the gods, it may also have the descriptor “divine” or “magical.” On the other hand, if it comes from her mutant genetic structure, then it has the descriptor “mutant.” A villain able to nullify mutant powers could potentially nullify all of the heroine’s powers!
modifiers.raw: Her Environment is the power to control the weather, giving it the descriptor “weather.” If the hero- ine’s power comes as a gift from the gods, it may also have the descriptor “divine” or “magical.” On the other hand, if it comes from her mutant genetic structure, then it has the descriptor “mutant.” A villain able to nullify mutant powers could potentially nullify all of the heroine’s powers!

modifiers.description: The number of power descriptors is virtually limitless.
modifiers.raw: The number of power descriptors is virtually limitless.

modifiers.description: The players and Gamemaster should cooperate to apply the appropriate descriptors to characters’ powers and use com- mon sense when dealing with how the different descrip- tors interact.
modifiers.raw: The players and Gamemaster should cooperate to apply the appropriate descriptors to characters’ powers and use com- mon sense when dealing with how the different descrip- tors interact.

modifiers.description: Just because one hero throws “lightning” and an opponent can absorb “electricity” doesn’t mean the vil- lain’s absorption doesn’t work because it’s not the exact same descriptor.
modifiers.raw: Just because one hero throws “lightning” and an opponent can absorb “electricity” doesn’t mean the vil- lain’s absorption doesn’t work because it’s not the exact same descriptor.

modifiers.description: A certain amount of flexibility is built into descriptors, allowing them to cover the full range of possible powers.
modifiers.raw: A certain amount of flexibility is built into descriptors, allowing them to cover the full range of possible powers.

modifiers.description: As in all things, the GM is the final arbitrator and should be consistent when ruling on whether or not a particular descriptor is appropri- ate and how all effects and descriptors interact in the series.
modifiers.raw: As in all things, the GM is the final arbitrator and should be consistent when ruling on whether or not a particular descriptor is appropri- ate and how all effects and descriptors interact in the series.

modifiers.description: The powers in this chapter provide numerous examples of descriptors.
modifiers.raw: The powers in this chapter provide numerous examples of descriptors.

modifiers.description: In this case, one character (usually the one with the high- est bonus) is considered the leader of the effort and makes the check normally, while each helper makes the same type of check using the same trait(s) against DC 10.
modifiers.raw: In this case, one character (usually the one with the high- est bonus) is considered the leader of the effort and makes the check normally, while each helper makes the same type of check using the same trait(s) against DC 10.

modifiers.description: One degree of failure provides no modifier, but two or more impose a –2 circumstance penalty!
modifiers.raw: One degree of failure provides no modifier, but two or more impose a –2 circumstance penalty!

modifiers.description: SENSORY DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK SENSE TYPES Senses in MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS are grouped into sense types, descriptors for how different sensory effects work.

modifiers.description: This is the catchall for other sensory descrip- tors not given above, including unusual senses or exotic descriptors like cosmic, gravitic, magical, and so forth.
modifiers.raw: This is the catchall for other sensory descrip- tors not given above, including unusual senses or exotic descriptors like cosmic, gravitic, magical, and so forth.

modifiers.raw: POWERS 147147 MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK DELUXE HERO’S HANDBOOK each other they must have opposed descriptors.

modifiers.description: In some cases, such as magical or mental effects, powers of the same descriptor can also counter each other.
modifiers.raw: In some cases, such as magical or mental effects, powers of the same descriptor can also counter each other.

modifiers.description: The GM is the final arbiter as to whether or not an effect with a particular descriptor can counter another.
modifiers.raw: The GM is the final arbiter as to whether or not an effect with a particular descriptor can counter another.

modifiers.description: The Nullify effect (see page 173) can counter any effect of a particular descriptor!
modifiers.raw: The Nullify effect (see page 173) can counter any effect of a particular descriptor!

modifiers.description: 1 point per rank EFFECT NAME TYPE You can impose some debilitating condition or conditions on a target by making a close attack.
modifiers.raw: 1 point per rank EFFECT NAME TYPE You can impose some debilitating condition or conditions on a target by making a close attack.

modifiers.description: Potential descriptors include coughing or sneez- ing, creeping mental influence, drowsiness, euphoria, fear, itchiness, lethargy, nausea, pain, or tipsiness.
modifiers.raw: Potential descriptors include coughing or sneez- ing, creeping mental influence, drowsiness, euphoria, fear, itchiness, lethargy, nausea, pain, or tipsiness.

modifiers.description: Potential descriptors include agonizing pain, confusion, ecstasy, momentary emotional or men- tal influence, paralysis, seizure, terror, or vomiting.
modifiers.raw: Potential descriptors include agonizing pain, confusion, ecstasy, momentary emotional or men- tal influence, paralysis, seizure, terror, or vomiting.

modifiers.description: The exact nature and descriptors of the Affliction are up to you, chosen when you acquire the effect, with the GM’s approval;
modifiers.raw: The exact nature and descriptors of the Affliction are up to you, chosen when you acquire the effect, with the GM’s approval;

modifiers.description: This modifier causes an Affliction to increase incrementally without any effort from you.
modifiers.raw: This modifier causes an Affliction to increase incrementally without any effort from you.

modifiers.description: 196), the target of an Affliction effect with this modifier recovers automatically, no check required, at the end of You can transform into something other than mere flesh and blood, from a body of organic steel to a cloud of gas, a mass of liquid, a swarm of tiny insect-sized robots, or anything else you want to develop.
modifiers.raw: 196), the target of an Affliction effect with this modifier recovers automatically, no check required, at the end of You can transform into something other than mere flesh and blood, from a body of organic steel to a cloud of gas, a mass of liquid, a swarm of tiny insect-sized robots, or anything else you want to develop.

modifiers.description: Choose a set of effects that reflect the capabilities of your Alternate Form, based on the examples following.
modifiers.raw: Choose a set of effects that reflect the capabilities of your Alternate Form, based on the examples following.

modifiers.description: Then choose the action required to assume your Alternate Form:
modifiers.raw: Then choose the action required to assume your Alternate Form:

modifiers.description: Some potential Alternate Forms (and their possible effects) include:
modifiers.raw: Some potential Alternate Forms (and their possible effects) include:

modifiers.description: Essentially, all your powers have the Activa- tion modifier!
modifiers.raw: Essentially, all your powers have the Activa- tion modifier!

modifiers.description: BASIC CONDITIONS Each basic condition describes a single game modifier.
modifiers.raw: BASIC CONDITIONS Each basic condition describes a single game modifier.

modifiers.description: For an additional flaw, you can only comprehend a narrow type of subject (dogs, falcons, or dolphins, for example).
modifiers.raw: For an additional flaw, you can only comprehend a narrow type of subject (dogs, falcons, or dolphins, for example).

modifiers.description: 2 points per rank You gain total concealment from a particular sense while this effect is active, although you are still detectable to other senses (even other senses of the same sense type;
modifiers.raw: 2 points per rank You gain total concealment from a particular sense while this effect is active, although you are still detectable to other senses (even other senses of the same sense type;

modifiers.description: so you could have full concealment against normal sight, but not infravision or any other sense in the sight sense type).
modifiers.raw: so you could have full concealment against normal sight, but not infravision or any other sense in the sight sense type).

modifiers.description: This modifier allows you to grant Conceal- ment to others while you are touching them, or at range, if you also apply the Range modifier.
modifiers.raw: This modifier allows you to grant Conceal- ment to others while you are touching them, or at range, if you also apply the Range modifier.

modifiers.description: To only affect some targets in the area, apply the Selective modifier as well.
modifiers.raw: To only affect some targets in the area, apply the Selective modifier as well.

modifiers.description: One ex- ample is Limited to Machines, where your Concealment only fools senses with a technological descriptor.
modifiers.raw: One ex- ample is Limited to Machines, where your Concealment only fools senses with a technological descriptor.

modifiers.description: They may be made of solidified energy, “hardened” water or air, transmuted bulk matter, ice, stone, or some other medium, depending on the effect’s descriptors.
modifiers.raw: They may be made of solidified energy, “hardened” water or air, transmuted bulk matter, ice, stone, or some other medium, depending on the effect’s descriptors.

modifiers.description: They can be solid or hollow, opaque or transparent, as you choose when you use the effect, limited by your descriptors and the Gamemaster’s judgment.
modifiers.raw: They can be solid or hollow, opaque or transparent, as you choose when you use the effect, limited by your descriptors and the Gamemaster’s judgment.

modifiers.description: The Selective modifier allows you to vary the cover and concealment your objects provide.
modifiers.raw: The Selective modifier allows you to vary the cover and concealment your objects provide.

modifiers.description: Imposing conditions on the target other than just trapping them requires a separate effect, such as Affliction (see Afflic- tion in this chapter), which you may wish to acquire as an Alternate Effect of Create (see Alternate Effect in this chapter).
modifiers.raw: Imposing conditions on the target other than just trapping them requires a separate effect, such as Affliction (see Afflic- tion in this chapter), which you may wish to acquire as an Alternate Effect of Create (see Alternate Effect in this chapter).

modifiers.description: They resist being moved with a Strength rank equal to the modifier rank.
modifiers.raw: They resist being moved with a Strength rank equal to the modifier rank.

modifiers.description: This modifier either makes created objects not noticeable as constructs for 1 rank (they look just like real objects) or not noticeable at all for 2 ranks (such as objects composed of invisible force).
modifiers.raw: This modifier either makes created objects not noticeable as constructs for 1 rank (they look just like real objects) or not noticeable at all for 2 ranks (such as objects composed of invisible force).

modifiers.description: apply the Extra Condition modifier for each ad- ditional sense affected.
modifiers.raw: apply the Extra Condition modifier for each ad- ditional sense affected.

modifiers.description: see the Alternate Effect modi- fier for details.
modifiers.raw: see the Alternate Effect modi- fier for details.

modifiers.description: If you have it at a lower rank, create your duplicate as a scaled-down version of yourself, with a power level equal to your rank in this power and starting power points determined accordingly (power rank x 15).
modifiers.raw: If you have it at a lower rank, create your duplicate as a scaled-down version of yourself, with a power level equal to your rank in this power and starting power points determined accordingly (power rank x 15).

modifiers.description: So a power level 11 hero who has Duplication 8 creates a power level 8 “duplicate” with (8 x 15) 120 power points and proportionately lower-ranked traits.
modifiers.raw: So a power level 11 hero who has Duplication 8 creates a power level 8 “duplicate” with (8 x 15) 120 power points and proportionately lower-ranked traits.

modifiers.description: Deflect modifiers are limited by power level.
modifiers.raw: Deflect modifiers are limited by power level.

modifiers.description: Like a ranged attack, if you Deflect at medium range, you have a –2 circumstance modifier on your check.
modifiers.raw: Like a ranged attack, if you Deflect at medium range, you have a –2 circumstance modifier on your check.

modifiers.description: At long range, you have a –5 circumstance modifier.
modifiers.raw: At long range, you have a –5 circumstance modifier.

modifiers.description: To create a kind of “deflec- tion field” or similar effect that automatically deflects attacks over a wide area, use an Enhanced Dodge and/or Enhanced Parry effect with modifiers like Area and Selective.
modifiers.raw: To create a kind of “deflec- tion field” or similar effect that automatically deflects attacks over a wide area, use an Enhanced Dodge and/or Enhanced Parry effect with modifiers like Area and Selective.

modifiers.description: First, you must successfully deflect the attack, then make a normal attack check using your own attack modifier to hit with the reflected attack.
modifiers.raw: First, you must successfully deflect the attack, then make a normal attack check using your own attack modifier to hit with the reflected attack.

modifiers.description: Element Control is further refined with various Alter- nate Effects (see Alternate Effects, page 188), expand- ing what you can do with your control.
modifiers.raw: Element Control is further refined with various Alter- nate Effects (see Alternate Effects, page 188), expand- ing what you can do with your control.

modifiers.description: Alternate Effects you do not add to your power as full-fledged modifiers are still available to you as power stunts using extra ef- fort (see Powers Stunts, page 20).
modifiers.raw: Alternate Effects you do not add to your power as full-fledged modifiers are still available to you as power stunts using extra ef- fort (see Powers Stunts, page 20).

modifiers.description: Your enhanced trait is still subject to power level limits, so your unenhanced rank must be be- low the limit by at least the amount of the enhancement to accommodate it.
modifiers.raw: Your enhanced trait is still subject to power level limits, so your unenhanced rank must be be- low the limit by at least the amount of the enhancement to accommodate it.

modifiers.description: If your Aura damages some targets but not others, apply the Selective or Lim- ited modifiers (depending on whether or not the selec- tivity is under your control).
modifiers.raw: If your Aura damages some targets but not others, apply the Selective or Lim- ited modifiers (depending on whether or not the selec- tivity is under your control).

modifiers.description: Energy Control is further defined by the addition of Alter- nate Effects (see Alternate Effects, page 188), expand- ing what you can do with your control.
modifiers.raw: Energy Control is further defined by the addition of Alter- nate Effects (see Alternate Effects, page 188), expand- ing what you can do with your control.

modifiers.description: Add as many Alternate Effects to your Energy Control as you can afford, and consider some additional ones as options for power stunts (see Powers Stunts, page 20).
modifiers.raw: Add as many Alternate Effects to your Energy Control as you can afford, and consider some additional ones as options for power stunts (see Powers Stunts, page 20).

modifiers.description: If you have one you can acquire others as Alternate Effects, but you can then only use and maintain one at a time.
modifiers.raw: If you have one you can acquire others as Alternate Effects, but you can then only use and maintain one at a time.

modifiers.description: To use or maintain multiple Environment effects simultane- ously, add their costs together for the effect’s total cost per rank or apply the Selective modifier, allowing you to mix-and-match effects.
modifiers.raw: To use or maintain multiple Environment effects simultane- ously, add their costs together for the effect’s total cost per rank or apply the Selective modifier, allowing you to mix-and-match effects.

modifiers.description: Enhanced Trait, Fades, Reaction • as base trait You take the energy from a particular type of attack, chosen when you take this power, and use it to en- hance one of your traits.
modifiers.raw: Enhanced Trait, Fades, Reaction • as base trait You take the energy from a particular type of attack, chosen when you take this power, and use it to en- hance one of your traits.

modifiers.description: Typically this is either Strength or a Ranged Damage effect of the same energy type as the initial attack, but other traits are possible, including Quickness, Regeneration, Speed, or the like.
modifiers.raw: Typically this is either Strength or a Ranged Damage effect of the same energy type as the initial attack, but other traits are possible, including Quickness, Regeneration, Speed, or the like.

modifiers.description: Absorption often includes the additional ef- fect of Protection (Impervious and often Limited to the absorbed type of attack) or even Immunity to the ab- sorbed attack.
modifiers.raw: Absorption often includes the additional ef- fect of Protection (Impervious and often Limited to the absorbed type of attack) or even Immunity to the ab- sorbed attack.

modifiers.description: Depending on your descriptors, you may also apply cir- cumstance modifiers to Acrobatics and Athletics checks for surface conditions.
modifiers.raw: Depending on your descriptors, you may also apply cir- cumstance modifiers to Acrobatics and Athletics checks for surface conditions.

modifiers.description: Power effects with the dark- ness descriptor may be countered with a successful power check (see Countering Effects, page 148).
modifiers.raw: Power effects with the dark- ness descriptor may be countered with a successful power check (see Countering Effects, page 148).

modifiers.description: VISIBILITY You impose a -2 modifier to Perception checks for 1 point per rank, and a -5 for 2 points per rank.
modifiers.raw: VISIBILITY You impose a -2 modifier to Perception checks for 1 point per rank, and a -5 for 2 points per rank.

modifiers.description: If you apply all of your limbs to a grab attempt (rather than taking the option to leave some of them free), you gain a UNDER THE HOOD:
modifiers.raw: If you apply all of your limbs to a grab attempt (rather than taking the option to leave some of them free), you gain a UNDER THE HOOD:

modifiers.description: As an option for including some combat benefits with Extra Limbs, consider allowing the application of the Multiattack extra to the Strength of a character with Extra Limbs, reflecting the ability to launch a flurry of attacks at a single opponent, or to “spread” those attacks among a number of nearby opponents.
modifiers.raw: As an option for including some combat benefits with Extra Limbs, consider allowing the application of the Multiattack extra to the Strength of a character with Extra Limbs, reflecting the ability to launch a flurry of attacks at a single opponent, or to “spread” those attacks among a number of nearby opponents.

modifiers.description: Your Extra Limbs are merely a projection of your power rather than an extension of your body.
modifiers.raw: Your Extra Limbs are merely a projection of your power rather than an extension of your body.

modifiers.description: It’s likely Extra Limbs with this extra are not eligible for the Innate modifier.
modifiers.raw: It’s likely Extra Limbs with this extra are not eligible for the Innate modifier.

modifiers.description: generally, if something has no real game effect, it’s just a descriptor.
modifiers.raw: generally, if something has no real game effect, it’s just a descriptor.

modifiers.description: The Feature effect is intended to round out various minor traits and abilities characters might have, but it is entirely optional and not meant to burden MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS character design with needless amounts of detail.
modifiers.raw: The Feature effect is intended to round out various minor traits and abilities characters might have, but it is entirely optional and not meant to burden MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS character design with needless amounts of detail.

modifiers.description: It’s for traits with an actual game effect, not merely descriptors or background color (neither of which should cost any points).
modifiers.raw: It’s for traits with an actual game effect, not merely descriptors or background color (neither of which should cost any points).

modifiers.description: This is the Swimming power as an Alternate Effect.
modifiers.raw: This is the Swimming power as an Alternate Effect.

modifiers.description: As a sustained effect, your Force Field is noticeable, unless you apply the Subtle modifier.
modifiers.raw: As a sustained effect, your Force Field is noticeable, unless you apply the Subtle modifier.

modifiers.description: maintain Flight as a kind of “safety net,” your choice when you apply the modifier.
modifiers.raw: maintain Flight as a kind of “safety net,” your choice when you apply the modifier.

modifiers.description: This modifier reduces, and then elimi- nates, these traces.
modifiers.raw: This modifier reduces, and then elimi- nates, these traces.

modifiers.description: Growth modifiers are restricted by power level limits.
modifiers.raw: Growth modifiers are restricted by power level limits.

modifiers.description: To heal multiple subjects at once, apply the Area modifier.
modifiers.raw: To heal multiple subjects at once, apply the Area modifier.

modifiers.description: Your Healing can remove even Incurable ef- fects (see the Incurable modifier).
modifiers.raw: Your Healing can remove even Incurable ef- fects (see the Incurable modifier).

modifiers.description: One Type of Damage (such as energy or blud- geoning damage), Objects (in conjunction with Affects Objects), Others (you can’t use Healing on yourself), or Self (you can only use Healing on yourself).
modifiers.raw: One Type of Damage (such as energy or blud- geoning damage), Objects (in conjunction with Affects Objects), Others (you can’t use Healing on yourself), or Self (you can only use Healing on yourself).

modifiers.description: COMBINED CONDITIONS Combined conditions are sets of basic conditions tied to a common descriptor.
modifiers.raw: COMBINED CONDITIONS Combined conditions are sets of basic conditions tied to a common descriptor.

modifiers.description: aging, disease, poison, one environmental condition (cold, heat, high pressure, radiation, or vacuum), one type of suffocation (breathe normally underwater or in an alien atmosphere, for example), starvation and thirst, need for sleep, or a rare power descriptor (such as your own powers, a close sibling’s powers, etc.).
modifiers.raw: aging, disease, poison, one environmental condition (cold, heat, high pressure, radiation, or vacuum), one type of suffocation (breathe normally underwater or in an alien atmosphere, for example), starvation and thirst, need for sleep, or a rare power descriptor (such as your own powers, a close sibling’s powers, etc.).

modifiers.description: INSUBSTANTIAL DESCRIPTORS Note that the fluid, gaseous, etc., rank names are them- selves essentially descriptors for the different Insubstan- tial effects.
modifiers.raw: INSUBSTANTIAL DESCRIPTORS Note that the fluid, gaseous, etc., rank names are them- selves essentially descriptors for the different Insubstan- tial effects.

modifiers.description: This modifier allows you to extend your Insubstantial effect to another character by touch, taking them Insubstantial with you.
modifiers.raw: This modifier allows you to extend your Insubstantial effect to another character by touch, taking them Insubstantial with you.

modifiers.description: This modifier is most effective for ranks 2 through 4, since the victim loses some or all ability to interact with the physical world.
modifiers.raw: This modifier is most effective for ranks 2 through 4, since the victim loses some or all ability to interact with the physical world.

modifiers.description: The default resistance for an Insubstantial Attack is Dodge, although it can be Fortitude or Will, as best suits the effect’s descriptors.
modifiers.raw: The default resistance for an Insubstantial Attack is Dodge, although it can be Fortitude or Will, as best suits the effect’s descriptors.

modifiers.description: Use this modifier if your character’s form is natu- rally or innately Insubstantial, particularly if the effect is permanent in duration.
modifiers.raw: Use this modifier if your character’s form is natu- rally or innately Insubstantial, particularly if the effect is permanent in duration.

modifiers.description: This modifier allows you to selectively make some portions of your body insubstantial while keeping others substantial (or vice versa).
modifiers.raw: This modifier allows you to selectively make some portions of your body insubstantial while keeping others substantial (or vice versa).

modifiers.description: At the GM’s option, applying the Action extra to use Insubstantial as a reaction allows you to switch forms “reflexively” in response to such hazards, even if it is not your turn.
modifiers.raw: At the GM’s option, applying the Action extra to use Insubstantial as a reaction allows you to switch forms “reflexively” in response to such hazards, even if it is not your turn.

modifiers.description: Others do not mimic other people but instead mimic the traits of animals (substi- tuting the “animal” descriptor for “another character”).
modifiers.raw: Others do not mimic other people but instead mimic the traits of animals (substi- tuting the “animal” descriptor for “another character”).

modifiers.description: Subtle Mind Reading is less detectable, requiring a DC 20 Perception check for either type of character to sense it, while two ranks of the Subtle modifier makes your Mind Reading completely undetectable.
modifiers.raw: Subtle Mind Reading is less detectable, requiring a DC 20 Perception check for either type of character to sense it, while two ranks of the Subtle modifier makes your Mind Reading completely undetectable.

modifiers.description: The benefits of extra effort are not limited by power level due to their extraordinary nature.
modifiers.raw: The benefits of extra effort are not limited by power level due to their extraordinary nature.

modifiers.description: This bonus can also negate a penalty (–2 circumstance penalty), allowing you to perform the check with no modifier, or reduce a major penalty from a –5 penalty to a –2 penalty.
modifiers.raw: This bonus can also negate a penalty (–2 circumstance penalty), allowing you to perform the check with no modifier, or reduce a major penalty from a –5 penalty to a –2 penalty.

modifiers.description: This modifier allows you to have an alternate set of traits, essentially a complete alternate character you change into, one set of traits per rank in Metamorph.
modifiers.raw: This modifier allows you to have an alternate set of traits, essentially a complete alternate character you change into, one set of traits per rank in Metamorph.

modifiers.description: Your other form(s) must have the same point total as you and are sub- ject to the same power level limits.
modifiers.raw: Your other form(s) must have the same point total as you and are sub- ject to the same power level limits.

modifiers.description: For example, if you can only Morph into humanoid forms, then your alternate forms all have to be humanoid.
modifiers.raw: For example, if you can only Morph into humanoid forms, then your alternate forms all have to be humanoid.

modifiers.description: Metamorph is best suited to characters with defined sets of alternate traits.
modifiers.raw: Metamorph is best suited to characters with defined sets of alternate traits.

modifiers.description: This effect can move objects, but cannot perform tasks of fine manipulation (like untying knots, typing, or ma- nipulating controls) without the Precise modifier.
modifiers.raw: This effect can move objects, but cannot perform tasks of fine manipulation (like untying knots, typing, or ma- nipulating controls) without the Precise modifier.

modifiers.description: Move Object with this modifier can be used for tasks involving fine manipulation.
modifiers.raw: Move Object with this modifier can be used for tasks involving fine manipulation.

modifiers.description: Apply the Subtle modifier for a less noticeable Move Object effect, such as invisible “psychokinesis” (which is generally also Perception Range).
modifiers.raw: Apply the Subtle modifier for a less noticeable Move Object effect, such as invisible “psychokinesis” (which is generally also Perception Range).

modifiers.description: If you apply the Increased Mass modi- fier, you can carry additional mass up to your modifier rank.
modifiers.raw: If you apply the Increased Mass modi- fier, you can carry additional mass up to your modifier rank.

modifiers.description: There is no difference in cost, but to be able to do both, take one Area Nullify as an Alternate Effect of the other.
modifiers.raw: There is no difference in cost, but to be able to do both, take one Area Nullify as an Alternate Effect of the other.

modifiers.description: Broad Nullify can counter effects of a particularly broad descriptor like magical, mutant, or technological effects.
modifiers.raw: Broad Nullify can counter effects of a particularly broad descriptor like magical, mutant, or technological effects.

modifiers.description: This modifier is available only with the Gamemas- ter’s permission and may depend on the effects available in the series.
modifiers.raw: This modifier is available only with the Gamemas- ter’s permission and may depend on the effects available in the series.

modifiers.description: Simultaneous Nullify can counter all ef- fects of a particular descriptor (such as fire or magic) at once.
modifiers.raw: Simultaneous Nullify can counter all ef- fects of a particular descriptor (such as fire or magic) at once.

modifiers.description: If you can Nullify multiple effects, this modifier al- lows you to choose which are nullified and which are not.
modifiers.raw: If you can Nullify multiple effects, this modifier al- lows you to choose which are nullified and which are not.

modifiers.description: If this modifier is applied to Concentration Nullify, keeping the countered effect(s) suppressed is only a free action for you each turn.
modifiers.raw: If this modifier is applied to Concentration Nullify, keeping the countered effect(s) suppressed is only a free action for you each turn.

modifiers.description: For 5 ranks, the sense type ignores all Concealment effects, regardless of descriptor.
modifiers.raw: For 5 ranks, the sense type ignores all Concealment effects, regardless of descriptor.

modifiers.description: COUNTERS ILLUSION 2 RANKS A sense type with this trait ignores the Illusion effect;
modifiers.raw: COUNTERS ILLUSION 2 RANKS A sense type with this trait ignores the Illusion effect;

modifiers.description: For 2 ranks you can detect things at range (with the normal –1 per 10 feet modifier to your Perception check).
modifiers.raw: For 2 ranks you can detect things at range (with the normal –1 per 10 feet modifier to your Perception check).

modifiers.description: Each additional time you apply this option, your range increases by an additional factor of 10, so 1 rank means you have a –1 to Perception checks per 100 feet, 2 ranks makes it –1 per 1,000 feet, and so on.
modifiers.raw: Each additional time you apply this option, your range increases by an additional factor of 10, so 1 rank means you have a –1 to Perception checks per 100 feet, 2 ranks makes it –1 per 1,000 feet, and so on.

modifiers.description: VISUAL Normal vision is ranged (with a –1/10 feet modifier), acute (able to distinguish fine details) and accurate (able to pinpoint the locations of things).
modifiers.raw: VISUAL Normal vision is ranged (with a –1/10 feet modifier), acute (able to distinguish fine details) and accurate (able to pinpoint the locations of things).

modifiers.raw: AUDITORY Normal hearing is ranged (with a –1/10 feet modifier), acute (able to pick up details like differences in tone), and radius (able to pick up on sounds coming from any direction).

modifiers.raw: OLFACTORY Normal human olfactory senses, which lump together smell and taste for descriptor purposes, are fairly lim- ited.

modifiers.description: MENTAL In MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS terms, the “sixth sense” or men- tal sense type is fairly crude in normal humans, lim- ited essentially to interactions with the Insight skill and awareness of mental effects used directly on you.
modifiers.raw: MENTAL In MUTANTS & MASTERMINDS terms, the “sixth sense” or men- tal sense type is fairly crude in normal humans, lim- ited essentially to interactions with the Insight skill and awareness of mental effects used directly on you.

modifiers.raw: That is, you do not have the option of using the modifier or not, it always applies.
